nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving freshwater streams, rivers and lakes with sandy or fine-gravel substrates that allow burrowing; the species is well documented from Lake Toba on Sumatra and from a wide range of lowland Southeast Asian waterways.

decorPreferences: soft sand or fine gravel substrate deep enough to burrow into (ideally 5–8 cm)
rocks, driftwood and live plants for cover
stable, well-cycled tank with hard, alkaline water to preserve shell integrity

diet.notes: Predatory snail used widely for biological control of pest snails (Pond, Bladder, Ramshorn and Malaysian Trumpet snails). Will also scavenge dead shrimp, deceased fish, soft tissue, fish eggs and newborn fry. Generally will NOT attack larger snails with operculate or thick shells such as adult Mystery/Apple snails and Nerites. Once pest snail populations are depleted, supplementary protein foods (frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, sinking carnivore wafers, gel foods) are commonly used to keep them fed.

goodWith: small peaceful community fish (tetras, rasboras, danios, guppies)
Mystery/Apple snails (too large to be attacked)
Nerite snails (too thick-shelled to be attacked)
avoidWith: ornamental freshwater snails you want to keep (Ramshorn, Bladder, Pond, Sulawesi Rabbit snails)
any tankmate likely to attack soft-bodied invertebrates

summary: Small (~2–3 cm) freshwater predatory snail from Southeast Asia, widely kept as a biological control for nuisance pest snails. Burrows in sand by day and hunts at night, but generally ignores larger Mystery and Nerite snails.
careNotes: Assassin Snails are generally considered one of the easiest freshwater invertebrates to keep, but their carnivorous nature shapes every husbandry decision. They are obligate predators of other snails and will systematically work through Pond, Bladder, Ramshorn and Malaysian Trumpet snail populations — making them the standard hobbyist answer to a pest-snail outbreak. They will also scavenge any dead shrimp, fish or other soft tissue in the tank. Once pest snails are gone, they must be fed protein-rich sinking foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, carnivore wafers) or they will slowly starve; they consume no algae and the algaeTypesConsumed list is intentionally empty. A soft, fine substrate (sand or fine gravel at least ~5 cm deep) is important because they spend most of the day buried, extending only the siphon, and emerge to hunt at night. Hard, alkaline water (pH ~7.0–8.0, gH ~8–15) helps preserve shell integrity; soft acidic water causes visible pitting and shell erosion, and crushed coral or mineral supplements are often added in soft-water regions. Like all aquatic invertebrates they are highly copper-sensitive — Fishkeeping World explicitly notes copper is toxic — so copper-based snail/parasite medications and copper-rich plant fertilisers must be avoided. Note on naming: 'Bumblebee Snail' is a common trade name for this freshwater species, but the same name is also used for the unrelated saltwater whelk Engina mendicaria; confirm water type before purchase. Sources broadly agree on the high-stakes fields: temperature was averaged across Aquarium Co-Op (21–27°C), Aqua Imports (22–26°C) and Fishkeeping World (21–28°C); pH was averaged across Aquarium Co-Op (7.2–8.0), Aqua Imports (6.5–7.5) and Fishkeeping World (7.0–8.0). For tank size, retail sources cite as little as 2 US gallons (~8 L) per snail, but Fishkeeping World recommends a 10 US gallon (~38 L) minimum to give a small colony enough substrate and stable parameters — the larger figure is used here and rounded to 40 L.
breedingNotes: One of the few aquarium snails that is NOT a self-fertilising hermaphrodite; sexes are separate, sexing visually is essentially impossible, and a group of around six or more is generally recommended to virtually guarantee both sexes are present. Females deposit single translucent, square egg capsules with pointy corners — each containing one yellow egg — on hard surfaces (glass, rocks, driftwood, plant leaves). Eggs typically hatch in roughly 3–8 weeks depending on temperature, and juveniles bury into the substrate where they remain mostly hidden until larger. Reproduction is slow compared with the pest snails they prey on, which is part of why they are considered safe to add to a tank without overrunning it.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (lists both Clea helena and Anentome helena), adult size (~1 in / 2.5 cm), temperature (22–26°C) and pH (6.5–7.5). Tank-size guidance (2 US gal per snail) treated as a per-snail floor rather than a community minimum.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed family Nassariidae, adult size 0.8–1.25 in (2–3 cm), lifespan up to 3 years, water parameters (21–28°C, pH 7.0–8.0, gH 8–15), and explicitly that copper is toxic to assassin snails. Also provided the larger tank-size figure (10 US gal / ~38 L) used as the basis for the 40 L minimum.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Anentome helena
additional.notes: Taxonomic confirmation only: accepted name Anentome helena (von dem Busch, 1847), older synonym Clea helena, family Nassariidae, order Neogastropoda. Also confirmed predominantly nocturnal, buries in substrate by day, native to Thailand/Malaysia/Sumatra including Lake Toba.
